mainText:
Good morning. Here's your Breeze communications briefing for Friday, March 27.

Starting with Breeze coverage.
No new Breeze coverage in the last 24 hours.

Now for industry and competitor news.
The DHS shutdown is now the dominant story in U.S. aviation, touching every part of the industry. The partial shutdown, which began February fourteenth, has left more than fifty thousand T.S.A. officers working without pay. As of Wednesday, more than four hundred fifty agents have resigned and absentee rates are running above eleven percent nationally — exceeding forty percent at some hubs including Atlanta, JFK, Houston, and New Orleans. The T.S.A. administrator warned Congress this week that staffing levels could force airport closures and threaten operations ahead of the 2026 FIFA World Cup this summer. BWI Airport closed security checkpoints on Thursday due to staffing gaps. Delta pulled its VIP escort and red coat services for members of Congress, with C.E.O. Ed Bastian calling the situation "inexcusable." The airline industry is also watching the political standoff closely — a Senate funding deal collapsed Wednesday after neither Democrats nor the White House could agree on terms, and Trump said he would sign an executive order to immediately pay T.S.A. agents, though details remain unclear. For Breeze, the question is whether this creates an operational drag on spring travel demand or whether the crisis resolves before it reaches that tipping point — and whether there is a proactive communications angle on how Breeze is managing the passenger experience at its airports during the disruption.

In the biggest competitive shakeup this week, Semafor reported on Tuesday that JetBlue has tapped advisers to evaluate selling itself to a rival airline. United Airlines, Southwest Airlines, and Alaska Airlines are the three carriers under scenario planning. The exploration is preliminary — no formal offers have been made — but JetBlue shares surged twelve percent on the news. JetBlue has not posted a full-year profit since 2019, and its stock is down over forty percent since the start of 2025 following the failed Spirit acquisition. Analysts note the current administration's openness to consolidation creates a narrow window, likely 2026 to 2027, for any deal to clear antitrust review. A United-JetBlue combination would give United domestic market share rivaling American and Delta, while Southwest-JetBlue would create roughly twenty-two percent domestic share. This is worth tracking closely — any consolidation among the big six directly reshapes the competitive environment Breeze operates in, and a JetBlue exit from certain markets could open route opportunities.

United Airlines reached a second tentative agreement with its flight attendants on Wednesday, with top-of-scale pay expected to exceed one hundred dollars per hour. The deal includes a seven hundred forty million dollar one-time payment to flight attendants, business-class hotel accommodations written into the contract, and restrictions on redeye assignments. The agreement still requires union leadership review and rank-and-file ratification, with new pay scales potentially taking effect as early as June. This sets a new benchmark for flight attendant compensation across the industry — worth watching for how it influences labor negotiations at other carriers and whether it adds cost pressure that shifts competitive dynamics.

That's your briefing. Roger is standing by when you're ready to take action on any of these stories.
